Motion For Summary Judgment (Part 2 Of 2 Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Aug-21-2018 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:
Defendants 1340 Hudson Avenue, L.P. and John Stewart Company, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment or, in the alternative, summary adjudication - (Part 2 of 2 of the Court's ruling)

Assuming without deciding that the Property Defendants presented sufficient facts to establish that they did not have the legal authority to tow Ms. Grayson's vehicle once the vehicle had been towed to their complex, the Property Defendants did not present any facts showing why they could not have refused to allow the vehicle to be towed to the complex and/or why the vehicle could not have been placed in the carport with its front end first and/or why the Property defendants could not have taken any other actions to minimize the risk of danger to Assad such as making it harder to access or move the vehicle and/or why the Property Defendants did not make a reasonable inspection of the vehicle (including the TSCI) to determine whether it could be accessed and moved by a child. As stated in Staats, all these issues are factual issues which are ordinarily not amenable to resolution on a summary motion. None of those issues are amenable to resolution on this motion for the simple and basic reason that the Property Defendants provided no facts about any of them. Nor have the Property Defendants provided sufficient admissible facts to satisfy their summary burden as to causation and, even if they had, there is a triable dispute whether the asserted breach of their duty to protect Assad from the risk posed by Ms. Grayson's vehicle was a substantial factor in causing Assad's death and/or the assertedly malfunctioning TSCI was a superseding cause. Defense counsel requests a ruling on the objections, the Court's ruling is set forth on the record. (please see the official court reporter's transcript) Order signed. (see order for full details)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication - Joint
Off Calendar Aug-21-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Sean P. Moriarty, Esq. of Law Offices of Cesari Werner and Moriarty, 650.991.5126 (ext 15), appearing for the Plaintiff;
Kevin M. Baldwin, Esq. of Hinshaw Law, 415.393.0136, appearing for the Defendants 1340 Hudson Avenue and John Stewart Company;
Lauren O. Miller, Esq. of Bowman and Brooke LLP, 408.951.4519, appearing for the Defendant Kia Motors America.

Ruling: ARGUED. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants 1340 Hudson Avenue, L.P. and John Stewart Company, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment or, in the alternative, summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. The threshold issue is how to frame the alleged duty, if any, the Property Defendants owed to Assad Gibson vis-a-vis defendant DeAngela Grayson's vehicle. Because the Property Defendants are the owner and operator of the apartment complex, they owed Assad and all other tenants a duty "to use reasonable care to keep the property in a reasonably safe condition," which entails the "use of reasonable care to discover any unsafe conditions and to repair, replace, or give adequate warning of anything that could be reasonably expected to harm others." (CACI 1001). This basic duty is enhanced and expanded as applied to the facts of this case for at least two reasons. First, "a greater degree of care is generally owed to children because of their lack of capacity to appreciate risks and avoid danger." (McDaniel v. Sunset Manor Co. (1990) 220 Cal.App.3d. 1, 7.) Second, San Francisco Transportation Code 5.1 explicitly provides that "wrecked" or "inoperative vehicles ' constitute an attractive nuisance creating a hazard to the health and safety of minors ' [T]he presence of ' [a] wrecked ' or inoperative vehicle ' on private property ' is hereby declared to constitute a public nuisance." Per a very recent case decided by the First District, the proper way to frame what duty, if any, the Property Defendants owed to Assad with respect to Ms. Grayson's vehicle is to ask the question whether the Property Defendants had a duty to protect Assad from the risk posed by Ms. Grayson's vehicle. (Staats v. Vintner's Golf Club, LLC (Cal. App. August 1, 2018) 2018 WL 3640830). Because it is reasonably foreseeable that the presence of a wrecked and inoperable vehicle on the premises of the apartment complex where a minor child lives could cause injury to the child and the imposition of a duty on the owner and operator of the complex to protect a child from that danger is supported by the "policy factors," as a matter of law the Property Defendants "owed a duty to protect ' [Assad] from the risk posed by" the presence of Ms. Grayson's vehicle on the premises they owned and operated. (Id). Once that duty is established, Staats further holds that "which actions the ' [Property Defendants] should have taken to minimize the risk (including the extent of reasonable inspections), whether the ' [Property Defendants] did take those actions, and whether any failure to do so proximately caused ' [Assad's death] are all questions for the trier of fact" and not amenable to resolution on a motion for summary judgment/adjudication. Because the Property Defendants' motion did not frame the duty issue in the correct way and narrowly limited it only to consideration whether they had a duty to tow the vehicle, the Property Defendants did not satisfy their summary motion burden of presenting sufficient facts to establish that they owed no duty to Assad. But even if they had properly characterized the duty they owed to Assad, Staats makes clear that the Property Defendants are unable to prevail on a no duty argument on a summary motion. (Part 1 of 2 of the Court's ruling)
